title: Variables and Data Types Core Check
description: Check your understanding of variable declarations, primitive types, strings, numbers, booleans, and absence values.
author: DailyCoder

questions.prompt: Which variable declaration keyword should be your default choice in modern JavaScript?
questions.explanation: Use const by default, then switch to let only when reassignment is needed. Avoid var in new code.

options.text: It can be redeclared in the same scope
It can be reassigned but not redeclared in the same scope
It must always hold a string

questions.explanation: let allows reassignment, but it prevents redeclaration in the same scope and is block-scoped.

questions.prompt: Which values are JavaScript primitive types? (Select all that apply.)
questions.explanation: string, number, and boolean are primitive types. object is a non-primitive reference type.
The typeof operator returns "string" for string values.

questions.prompt: Which syntax is a template literal?
questions.explanation: Template literals use backticks and support interpolation with ${}.

questions.prompt: Which method removes whitespace from both ends of a string?
questions.explanation: trim() returns a new string with whitespace removed from the beginning and end.

questions.prompt: Which value represents an intentional absence of value?
questions.explanation: null is commonly used when a developer intentionally wants to represent no value.

questions.prompt: JavaScript has separate primitive number types for integers and decimals.
questions.explanation: JavaScript uses one main number type for both integers and decimals.

questions.prompt: How should you check whether a value is `NaN`?
questions.explanation: NaN is not equal to itself, so use Number.isNaN() to check for it.

questions.prompt: Which values are falsy in JavaScript? (Select all that apply.)
questions.explanation: 0, an empty string, and null are falsy. The string "false" is truthy because it is non-empty.
